sections.content: This capstone project involves building an end-to-end AI/ML application for cricket match analytics and player performance prediction. The system will ingest structured cricket data (player statistics, match outcomes, batting averages, bowling economy rates), perform exploratory data analysis, engineer features from raw metrics, train multiple machine learning models (linear regression, random forests, and neural networks), and deploy an API that predicts match outcomes and player performance. The project encompasses the entire ML pipeline: data preprocessing with pandas and NumPy, exploratory visualization using Matplotlib and Seaborn, feature engineering and scaling, hyperparameter tuning with scikit-learn, model evaluation using cross-validation and appropriate metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score for classification; RMSE and R² for regression), and packaging the solution as a reproducible, production-ready application. This capstone demonstrates competency in applied machine learning, data engineering, model selection, and deployment—skills directly transferable to real-world industry projects in sports analytics, fintech, healthcare, and e-commerce.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: A cricket team entering an international tournament must analyze historical match data, understand player form, predict opponent strategies, and optimize team composition before each game. Just as a captain studies opposition bowling attacks, fielding patterns, and pitch conditions through scorecards and video analysis to make tactical decisions, your ML project studies historical cricket data to uncover patterns and predict future outcomes. The data preprocessing phase mirrors the captain's initial scouting—cleaning incomplete records, standardizing metrics across different formats (T20, ODI, Tests), and handling outliers (like ignoring a player's one exceptional inning caused by pitch conditions). Feature engineering parallels identifying which specific player strengths matter most: a bowler's economy rate in powerplay overs is more predictive than total career runs. Model training is like the team's practice sessions, trying different strategies and formations until the optimal approach emerges. Finally, the deployed API represents match-day execution—given live conditions and player form, it predicts probable outcomes with confidence intervals, just as analysts use data to forecast match victory probability before the coin toss. Understanding this parallel reveals why data quality, feature relevance, and iterative testing matter so profoundly in machine learning: garbage data produces poor predictions, just as a team with inaccurate scout reports makes tactical blunders.

items: Build complete ML pipeline: data ingestion, cleaning, exploration, preprocessing, modeling, and API deployment using industry-standard libraries and workflows.
Perform exploratory data analysis (EDA) using statistical summaries, correlation matrices, and visualizations to uncover patterns in cricket performance metrics.
Engineer meaningful features from raw data: create derived metrics (economy rate, strike rate, consistency indices) and apply transformations (normalization, binning, encoding).
Train, evaluate, and compare multiple models (linear models, tree-based ensembles, neural networks) using proper validation strategies (cross-fold validation, hold-out tests).
Implement production-ready code: error handling, logging, unit testing, configuration management, and documentation for team collaboration and deployment.
Deploy trained models as REST API endpoints using Flask or FastAPI, enabling real-time predictions on new cricket match data with latency and accuracy monitoring.
Data source: Minimum 500 cricket match records with player statistics (batting average, strike rate, economy rate, wickets), match metadata (venue, format, date), and outcomes.
EDA deliverable: Generate 8-10 visualizations (distribution plots, correlation heatmaps, time-series trends, categorical breakdowns) with written insights uncovering data patterns.
Feature engineering: Create 15+ engineered features including rolling averages (form over last 5 matches), interaction terms (pitcher-batter matchups), and categorical encodings (venue effects).
Model diversity: Train at least 3 model types (e.g., logistic regression, random forest classifier, neural network) with documented hyperparameter choices and justification.
Validation rigor: Implement 5-fold cross-validation, separate hold-out test set, and proper metric reporting (confusion matrices, ROC-AUC, calibration curves for classification).
API implementation: Create Flask/FastAPI endpoints for single prediction, batch prediction, and model metadata retrieval with input validation and error responses.
Code quality: Modular functions, comprehensive docstrings, type hints, exception handling for missing/invalid data, and unit tests covering edge cases.
Documentation: README with project overview, data dictionary, model explanations, API endpoint specifications, and usage examples for reproducibility.

sections.content: The system follows a layered modular architecture composed of distinct functional components that interact through well-defined interfaces. The data layer ingests raw cricket match records from CSV, JSON, or database connections using pandas DataFrames, ensuring consistent schema validation and error handling for malformed inputs. The preprocessing layer applies transformations: missing value imputation (mean/median for numeric features, mode for categorical), outlier detection using Z-score and IQR methods, categorical encoding (one-hot for nominal variables, ordinal encoding for ranked features), and feature scaling using StandardScaler or MinMaxScaler depending on model requirements. The feature engineering layer constructs domain-specific derived metrics: rolling statistics (average runs in last 5 innings), lag features (previous performance), interaction terms (bowler type vs batter weakness), and domain indicators (match format effects, home vs away venue encoding). The modeling layer instantiates, trains, and evaluates multiple algorithm classes with scikit-learn pipelines that chain preprocessing and model steps to prevent data leakage. Model selection employs cross-validation scores and learning curves to identify optimal hyperparameters, while the evaluation layer generates comprehensive metrics and comparison visualizations. The persistence layer serializes trained models and preprocessing fitted transformers using joblib to enable consistent inference on new data. Finally, the API layer wraps selected models in a Flask/FastAPI service that accepts structured input (player statistics, match context), applies identical preprocessing, generates predictions with confidence scores, and returns JSON responses with proper error handling. This modular design ensures testability, reusability, and straightforward transition from development notebook to production deployment.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: A professional cricket team's preparation for a tournament mirrors this architecture perfectly. The data layer is the recruitment team gathering player statistics, match videos, and opposition scouting reports into a centralized database. The preprocessing layer corresponds to the fitness and coaching staff: they standardize training regimens, remove anomalies (a player's poor form due to injury doesn't reflect true ability), and normalize player metrics across different roles—comparing a fast bowler's fitness metrics to a spinner's requires adjusted baselines. The feature engineering layer is the tactical analysis: combining basic stats into meaningful insights like 'average runs per powerplay over' or 'success rate against left-hand batsmen in home conditions'—raw statistics matter less than derived, context-aware metrics. The modeling layer represents trying different team compositions and strategies in practice matches: testing aggressive batting orders, varying bowling combinations, adjusting field placements to see which configuration performs best against different opponent types. Model selection is the selection committee choosing the final squad by analyzing cross-validation results (how does the team perform against diverse oppositions in warm-ups?). The persistence layer is the team sheet and match preparation manual documented before the tournament. The API layer is match-day execution: given pre-game conditions and player form, the documented strategy (API endpoint) is executed to produce optimal outcomes. This architecture's modularity mirrors how professional teams separate concerns: scouts don't train players, coaches don't make selection decisions, and analysts don't field the team—each layer's independence enables agility and quality.

# Project structure and architecture skeleton
# Directory layout:
# cricket_ml_project/
#   ├── data/
#   │   ├── raw/
#   │   │   └── cricket_matches_raw.csv
#   │   └── processed/
#   │       └── cricket_matches_processed.csv
#   ├── notebooks/
#   │   ├── 01_eda.ipynb
#   │   ├── 02_feature_engineering.ipynb
#   │   └── 03_modeling.ipynb
#   ├── src/
#   │   ├── __init__.py
#   │   ├── data_loader.py
#   │   ├── preprocessor.py
#   │   ├── feature_engineer.py
#   │   ├── model_trainer.py
#   │   └── predictor.py
#   ├── models/
#   │   ├── best_model.joblib
#   │   └── preprocessor.joblib
#   ├── tests/
#   │   ├── test_data_loader.py
#   │   ├── test_preprocessor.py
#   │   └── test_model.py
#   ├── api/
#   │   ├── app.py
#   │   └── schemas.py
#   ├── config.yaml
#   ├── requirements.txt
#   └── README.md

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split, cross_val_score
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler, LabelEncoder
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.neural_network import MLPClassifier
from sklearn.metrics import classification_report, confusion_matrix, roc_auc_score
import joblib
import logging
from typing import Tuple, Dict, Any
from dataclasses import dataclass

logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

@dataclass
class CricketMatchMetadata:
    """Represents a cricket match record for type safety."""
    match_id: str
    player_name: str
    team: str
    opponent_team: str
    venue: str
    match_format: str  # 'T20', 'ODI', 'Test'
    batting_avg: float
    strike_rate: float
    economy_rate: float
    bowling_average: float
    match_outcome: int  # 1 for win, 0 for loss

class CricketDataLoader:
    """Loads and validates cricket match data."""
    
    def __init__(self
sections.filepath:
str):
        self.filepath = filepath
        self.dataframe = None
    
    def load_data(self) -> pd.DataFrame:
        """Load CSV with error handling."""
        try:
            self.dataframe = pd.read_csv(self.filepath)
            logger.info(f"Loaded {len(self.dataframe)} records from {self.filepath}")
            return self.dataframe
        except FileNotFoundError:
            logger.error(f"File not found: {self.filepath}")
            raise
        except Exception as e:
            logger.error(f"Error loading data: {e}")
            raise
    
    def validate_schema(self) -> bool:
        """Check required columns exist."""
        required_cols = ['match_id', 'player_name', 'batting_avg', 'strike_rate',
                        'economy_rate', 'bowling_average', 'match_outcome']
        missing = [col for col in required_cols if col not in self.dataframe.columns]
        if missing:
            logger.error(f"Missing columns: {missing}")
            return False
        logger.info("Schema validation passed")
        return True

class CricketPreprocessor:
    """Handles data cleaning and preprocessing."""
    
    def __init__(self):
        self.scaler = StandardScaler()
        self.label_encoders = {}
    
    def handle_missing_values(self
sections.df:
pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
        """Impute missing values appropriately."""
        numeric_cols = df.select_dtypes(include=[np.number]).columns
        for col in numeric_cols:
            df[col].fillna(df[col].median(), inplace=True)
        
        categorical_cols = df.select_dtypes(include=['object']).columns
        for col in categorical_cols:
            df[col].fillna(df[col].mode()[0] if not df[col].mode().empty else 'Unknown', inplace=True)
        
        logger.info(f"Missing values handled in {len(numeric_cols)} numeric and {len(categorical_cols)} categorical columns")
        return df
    
    def remove_outliers(self

sections.threshold:
float = 3.0) -> pd.DataFrame:
        """Remove outliers using Z-score method."""
        numeric_cols = df.select_dtypes(include=[np.number]).columns
        initial_len = len(df)
        
        for col in numeric_cols:
            z_scores = np.abs((df[col] - df[col].mean()) / df[col].std())
            df = df[z_scores < threshold]
        
        removed = initial_len - len(df)
        logger.info(f"Removed {removed} outlier records")
        return df
    
    def encode_categorical(self

sections.fit:
bool = True) -> np.ndarray:
        """Scale numeric features to unit variance."""
        if fit:
            X_scaled = self.scaler.fit_transform(X)
        else:
            X_scaled = self.scaler.transform(X)
        logger.info("Features scaled using StandardScaler")
        return X_scaled

class CricketFeatureEngineer:
    """Creates domain-specific derived features."""
    
    @staticmethod
    def engineer_features(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
        """Create new features from existing columns."""
        df_engineered = df.copy()
        
        # Derived metrics based on cricket analytics
        df_engineered['batting_consistency'] = (
            df_engineered['batting_avg'] * df_engineered['strike_rate'] / 100
        )
        df_engineered['bowling_efficiency'] = (
            1 / (df_engineered['economy_rate'] + 0.1)  # Avoid division by zero
        )
        df_engineered['overall_performance_index'] = (
            df_engineered['batting_avg'] * 0.4 + 
            df_engineered['strike_rate'] * 0.3 + 
            df_engineered['bowling_efficiency'] * 0.3
        )
        
        logger.info(f"Created 3 new engineered features from raw metrics")
        return df_engineered

# Example usage
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Initialize components
    loader = CricketDataLoader('data/raw/cricket_matches.csv')
    preprocessor = CricketPreprocessor()
    feature_engineer = CricketFeatureEngineer()
    
    # Data pipeline
    # df = loader.load_data()
    # loader.validate_schema()
    # df = preprocessor.handle_missing_values(df)
    # df = preprocessor.remove_outliers(df)
    # df = feature_engineer.engineer_features(df)
    # df = preprocessor.encode_categorical(df, fit=True)
    
    logger.info("Architecture initialized and ready for Phase 1 implementation")

sections.content: Phase 1 focuses on implementing the complete data pipeline and training baseline models. This includes building a robust data loader that reads cricket match records and validates schema integrity, implementing a preprocessor that handles missing values, detects and removes outliers, and scales features appropriately, and creating a feature engineer that derives domain-specific metrics (batting consistency, bowling efficiency, performance indices) from raw statistics. The core modeling component trains three distinct algorithms—logistic regression for linear decision boundaries, random forest for non-linear patterns and feature importance, and a neural network for complex interactions—using a properly stratified train-test split to ensure balanced class distribution. Each model undergoes 5-fold cross-validation to estimate generalization performance, and comprehensive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, ROC-AUC) are computed to enable informed model comparison. This phase establishes reproducible baseline results against which later improvements are measured.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: A cricket team's preparation begins with Phase 1 fundamentals—selecting the right players and establishing their baseline fitness. Just as a coach assesses each player's basic skills (batting technique, bowling pace, fielding accuracy) before developing them further, Phase 1 assesses raw cricket data to understand which metrics predict match outcomes. The data pipeline mirrors player recruitment: loading player profiles (data loader), verifying they meet basic standards (preprocessing removes unrealistic statistics like a batter's 500+ strike rate), and extracting meaningful traits (feature engineering identifies that 'consistency = batting average × strike rate' better predicts performance than either stat alone). Training three different models parallels trying three different playing strategies: conservative batting (logistic regression uses linear relationships), tactical flexibility (random forest adapts patterns for different match contexts), and aggressive innovation (neural network discovers hidden interactions between player strengths). Cross-validation is like practice matches against diverse opposition: you don't judge a strategy's effectiveness from one friendly against a weak team; you test it across multiple challengers to confirm robustness. ROC-AUC scoring parallels a team's win rate across different tournament formats—a 0.85 AUC means the model correctly ranks match-winning chances 85% of the time, just as a team with strong fundamentals wins in 85% of predictable situations. This foundation phase proves that your selected metrics and models have merit before investing in optimization.

# Phase 1: Core Implementation with Cricket Data

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split, cross_val_score, StratifiedKFold
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler, LabelEncoder
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.neural_network import MLPClassifier
from sklearn.metrics import (
    classification_report, confusion_matrix, roc_auc_score, 
    roc_curve, auc, accuracy_score, precision_score, recall_score, f1_score
)
import joblib
import logging
from typing import Tuple, Dict

logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

class Phase1ModelTrainer:
    """Train and evaluate baseline models on cricket data."""

        self.results = {}
        self.scaler = StandardScaler()
        self.X_train = None
        self.X_test = None
        self.y_train = None
        self.y_test = None

4f}")

        results = {
            'model': model

4f}")
        logger.info(f"Top features: {sorted(zip(['batting_avg', 'strike_rate', 'economy_rate', 'bowling_average', 'batting_consistency', 'overall_performance_index'], model.feature_importances_), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)[:3]}")
        return results
    
    def train_neural_network(self) -> Dict[str, any]:
        """Train neural network classifier."""
        logger.info("Training Neural Network...")

            hidden_layer_sizes=(64, 32, 16), max_iter=500,

        # Cross-validation

        logger.info(f"Neural Network CV scores (ROC-AUC): {cv_scores}

4f}")

        results = {
            'model': model

ROC-AUC.results['roc_auc']:
4f}")
        return results
    
    def print_summary(self):
        """Print comprehensive comparison of all models."""
        print("\n" + "="*70)
        print("PHASE 1: BASELINE MODEL COMPARISON")
        print("="*70)
        
        for model_name, results in self.results.items():
            print(f"\n{model_name.upper()}:")
            print(f"  Accuracy:  {results['accuracy']:.4f}")
            print(f"  Precision: {results['precision']:.4f}")
            print(f"  Recall:    {results['recall']:.4f}")
            print(f"  F1-Score:  {results['f1']:.4f}")
            print(f"  ROC-AUC:   {results['roc_auc']:.4f}")
            print(f"  CV Mean:   {results['cv_mean']:.4f} (+/- {results['cv_std']:.4f})")
        
        best_model = max(self.results.items(), key=lambda x: x[1]['roc_auc'])
        print(f"\n🏆 BEST MODEL: {best_model[0].upper()} with ROC-AUC: {best_model[1]['roc_auc']:.4f}")
        print("="*70)

# Example Phase 1 execution
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Create synthetic cricket data for demonstration
    np.random.seed(42)
    n_samples = 300
    
    cricket_data = pd.DataFrame({
        'match_id': [f'MATCH_{i:03d}' for i in range(n_samples)]
ROC-AUC.player_name:
np.random.choice(
            ['Virat Kohli', 'Rohit Sharma', 'Jasprit Bumrah', 'Babar Azam', 'Steve Smith']
ROC-AUC.match_outcome:
np.random.choice([0, 1], n_samples, p=[0.45, 0.55])  # 55% win rate
    })
    
    # Initialize and run Phase 1
    trainer = Phase1ModelTrainer()
    trainer.prepare_data(cricket_data)
    trainer.train_logistic_regression()
    trainer.train_random_forest()
    trainer.train_neural_network()
    trainer.print_summary()
    
    # Save best model
    best_model_name = max(trainer.results.items(), key=lambda x: x[1]['roc_auc'])[0]
    joblib.dump(trainer.models[best_model_name], 'models/best_model_phase1.joblib')
    joblib.dump(trainer.scaler, 'models/scaler_phase1.joblib')
    logger.info(f"Phase 1 complete. Best model saved: {best_model_name}")

ROC-AUC.content: Phase 2 advances Phase 1's baseline by implementing sophisticated feature engineering, hyperparameter optimization, and ensemble methods. This phase creates advanced derived features including rolling statistics (5-match rolling average of batting performance), lag features (previous match outcome influence), interaction terms (bowler specialization against specific batter types), and contextual encodings (home vs away venue effects, format-specific performance indices). Hyperparameter tuning employs GridSearchCV or RandomizedSearchCV to optimize model parameters based on cross-validation scores, replacing the manual hyperparameters from Phase 1 with data-driven selections. Ensemble methods are introduced—stacking multiple models with a meta-learner to combine their strengths, voting classifiers that aggregate predictions from diverse base learners—to achieve performance beyond individual models. Feature selection techniques (recursive feature elimination, permutation importance) identify the most predictive variables, reducing model complexity and training time. Calibration curves ensure that predicted probabilities reflect true event likelihoods, critical for reliable confidence scores in deployment. This phase typically yields 5-15% performance improvements over baseline and produces a production-ready candidate model.

mean.content: 🏏 Think of it like cricket: If Phase 1 is a team's basic selection and formation, Phase 2 is tactical refinement before tournament competition. Just as a captain doesn't field the same 11 players and batting order for every match type—adjusting for fast pitches vs spinning tracks, home vs away conditions, opposition strengths—Phase 2 tunes models for specific prediction contexts. Creating rolling statistics parallels analyzing recent form: Rohit Sharma's 30-run average over his last 5 innings predicts his next performance better than career average, so features capturing current form (rolling averages) matter more than static metrics. Lag features mimic momentum: a batter who won the previous match has different confidence and setup than after a loss. Interaction features are like strategic partnerships: a slow-bowler's economy rate means little without knowing the opposing batting type (does opponent specialize against pace?), so combining 'bowler type × batter strength' creates meaningful predictive features. Hyperparameter tuning is like optimizing field placement for a specific batter: you don't use one standard field for all opposition—slip placement depends on batter's slip-ball tendencies, deep field on hitting patterns, so optimal parameters depend on data characteristics. Ensemble methods parallel India's playing XI composition: rather than relying on one star batter, combining multiple players with different strengths (aggressor, accumulator, finisher) creates robustness. A stacked ensemble is like a captain who relies on core council (batsman, bowler, wicket-keeper) to make final match decisions. Calibration ensures predictions match reality: if your model says 60% win probability, actual wins should occur 60% of the time, just as a team with stated confidence level should deliver proportional results. This phase transforms a proof-of-concept into a tournament-ready strategy.

ROC-AUC.content:
# Phase 2: Advanced Feature Engineering, Hyperparameter Tuning, and Ensemble Methods

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV, RandomizedSearchCV, cross_val_score, StratifiedKFold
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier, GradientBoostingClassifier, StackingClassifier, VotingClassifier
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.feature_selection import RFE, permutation_importance
from sklearn.metrics import roc_auc_score, classification_report, calibration_curve
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
import joblib
import logging
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

class CricketAdvancedFeatureEngineer:
    """Advanced feature engineering for cricket analytics."""
    
    @staticmethod
    def create_rolling_statistics(df: pd.DataFrame
ROC-AUC.window:
int = 5) -> pd.DataFrame:
        """
        Create rolling window statistics to capture recent form.
        E.g., 5-match rolling average of batting performance indicates current form.
        """
        df = df.sort_values('match_id').copy()
        
        # Group by player to compute rolling stats independently
        df['batting_avg_rolling'] = df.groupby('player_name')['batting_avg'].rolling(window=window).mean().reset_index(0, drop=True)
        df['strike_rate_rolling'] = df.groupby('player_name')['strike_rate'].rolling(window=window).mean().reset_index(0, drop=True)
        df['economy_rate_rolling'] = df.groupby('player_name')['economy_rate'].rolling(window=window).mean().reset_index(0, drop=True)
        
        # Forward fill for early matches with insufficient history
        df['batting_avg_rolling'].fillna(df['batting_avg'], inplace=True)
        df['strike_rate_rolling'].fillna(df['strike_rate'], inplace=True)
        df['economy_rate_rolling'].fillna(df['economy_rate'], inplace=True)
        
        logger.info(f"Created rolling statistics with window={window}")
        return df
    
    @staticmethod
    def create_lag_features(df: pd.DataFrame
ROC-AUC.lag:
int = 1) -> pd.DataFrame:
        """
        Create lagged features capturing momentum and recent outcome.
        Previous match outcome influences player confidence and approach.
        """
        df = df.sort_values('match_id').copy()
        
        df['match_outcome_lag'] = df.groupby('player_name')['match_outcome'].shift(lag)
        df['batting_avg_lag'] = df.groupby('player_name')['batting_avg'].shift(lag)
        
        df['match_outcome_lag'].fillna(0, inplace=True)
        df['batting_avg_lag'].fillna(df['batting_avg'].mean(), inplace=True)
        
        logger.info(f"Created lag features with lag={lag}")
        return df
    
    @staticmethod
    def create_interaction_features(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
        """
        Create interaction features for complex relationships.
        E.g., bowler's effectiveness (economy) × opponent batting strength.
        """
        # Bowling-batting matchup effectiveness
        df['bowler_batter_interaction'] = df['economy_rate'] / (df['strike_rate'] + 1)
        
        # Venue impact on performance
        df['venue_batting_index'] = df['batting_avg'] * (
            df['venue'].map({'MCG': 1.1

score.grid_search.best_score_:
4f}")
        
        best_model = grid_search.best_estimator_
        y_pred_proba = best_model.predict_proba(self.X_test)[:, 1]
        test_auc = roc_auc_score(self.y_test, y_pred_proba)
        
        self.best_models['random_forest'] = best_model
        self.results['random_forest'] = {
            'model': best_model

params.test_auc:
test_auc
        }
        
        logger.info(f"Voting Ensemble test AUC: {test_auc:.4f}")
        return self.results['voting']
    
    def print_summary(self):
        """Print Phase 2 results summary."""
        print("\n" + "="*70)
        print("PHASE 2: OPTIMIZED MODELS AND ENSEMBLES")
        print("="*70)
        
        for model_name, results in self.results.items():
            print(f"\n{model_name.upper()}:")
            print(f"  Test AUC: {results['test_auc']:.4f}")
            if 'best_params' in results:
                print(f"  Params: {results['best_params']}")
        
        best_model = max(self.results.items(), key=lambda x: x[1]['test_auc'])
        print(f"\n🏆 BEST PHASE 2 MODEL: {best_model[0].upper()} with AUC: {best_model[1]['test_auc']:.4f}")
        print("="*70)

# Example Phase 2 execution
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Assume cricket_data from Phase 1 is available
    # Create advanced features
    feature_engineer = CricketAdvancedFeatureEngineer()
    
    # This would be applied to actual data:
    # cricket_data = feature_engineer.create_rolling_statistics(cricket_data)
    # cricket_data = feature_engineer.create_lag_features(cricket_data)
    # cricket_data = feature_engineer.create_interaction_features(cricket_data)
    
    logger.info("Phase 2 feature engineering and ensemble optimization framework ready")

sections.content: Phase 3 transitions the optimized model from development to production deployment with comprehensive error handling, logging, validation, and testing. This phase implements robust error handling for missing inputs, invalid data types, and out-of-distribution predictions with graceful fallbacks and user-friendly error messages. Unit tests verify correctness of data loaders, preprocessors, feature engineers, and prediction pipelines across normal cases and edge cases (empty dataframes, single samples, extreme values). Integration tests ensure the full pipeline works end-to-end. Logging infrastructure tracks prediction requests, model performance metrics, and system health. The API layer (Flask/FastAPI) receives predictions requests, applies identical preprocessing as training, returns JSON responses with predictions and confidence intervals, and handles concurrent requests safely. Documentation includes architecture diagrams, API specifications, deployment instructions, and model interpretation guides so stakeholders understand predictions and limitations. Monitoring in production tracks prediction latency, model accuracy drift (comparing recent predictions to actual outcomes), data distribution shift (detecting when new data differs significantly from training data), and resource utilization. This phase ensures the model operates reliably in production, maintains performance over time, and provides transparency for business stakeholders.
